scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: Covers every key piece the rubric names — chat_completion.rb, function_dispatch.rb, function_tool_adapter.rb, message_adapters/base.rb, multimodal_content_adapter.rb, mcp/ tree, transcript_adapter.rb — with short role descriptions, so the agent can pick an entry point without fresh exploration. Slight gap: the answer buries the 'set of composable mixins' architecture in the final bullet rather than leading with it as the organizing frame.

specificity.rationale: File paths are named concretely (lib/raix/chat_completion.rb:454, function_dispatch.rb:132, mcp/{sse_client,stdio_client,tool}.rb), module names are fully qualified (Raix::ChatCompletion, Raix::FunctionDispatch), and DSL method names (function :name do) appear inline — all actionable for targeted follow-ups.

justification.rationale: The answer notes which mixins to include and for what purpose ('to declare functions', 'for remote tools') but does not explain WHY composable mixins were chosen over a base class — the 'a la carte behavior addition' rationale the rubric asks for is absent; the agent gets the WHAT without the design intent.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty is flagged anywhere — the answer presents mixin composition, dispatch flow, and MCP integration with uniform confidence; the agent has no signal about which parts to verify before acting.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full round-trip with file:line at every layer: entry at chat_completion.rb:73, model request at :147/:340, tool-call detection at :160–:161, dispatch through :186→:297→:298 (function_dispatch.rb), result append at function_dispatch.rb:80–:101, and recursion with max_tool_calls decrement at :200–:209. An agent could open the file at any of these lines and start editing with no exploratory warm-up.

specificity.rationale: Every named piece is exact — chat_completion, ruby_llm_request, dispatch_tool_function, stop_tool_calls_and_respond!, max_tool_calls — each with file:line, not vague phrases like 'the chat loop'. The one slight gap is that the answer doesn't name the method that appends the tool message in function_dispatch.rb, only the line range.

justification.rationale: The answer explains mechanism (recursion decrements max_tool_calls, loop repeats until plain message) but not the design rationale — it never explains WHY results re-enter via transcript append (so the full conversation history reaches the next model request) or WHY recursion is used instead of iteration. The numbered steps read as 'what happens' rather than 'why it works this way'.

uncertainty.rationale: The three key branches — max_tool_calls guard, multiple tool calls in one response, stop_tool_calls_and_respond! — are all named with line numbers, but none are flagged as 'verify this before changing it'; the answer presents them as established fact without hedging on whether its line numbers are exact or whether the dispatch path differs for MCP tools.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces every stage with file:line: declaration DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46, @functions storage at :47-48, tools wrapping at :126-130, FunctionToolAdapter.convert_tools_for_ruby_llm at function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57 building RubyLLM::Tool subclasses, and available_tools/filtered_tools filtering at chat_completion.rb:73/:303-313 — the agent can add a new declared capability and knows exactly where each piece lives.

specificity.rationale: Every named piece has a concrete file:line: function DSL (:46), attr_reader :functions (:27), tools method (:126-130), FunctionToolAdapter (:50-57), create_tool_from_function (:6), filtered_tools (:303-313), UndeclaredToolError (:310) — nothing is described vaguely.

justification.rationale: The answer describes the mechanism faithfully but never states WHY the design is a class-level DSL (so host classes declare capabilities declaratively and they are automatically serialized into the schema sent to the model); the design rationale is implicit rather than articulated.

uncertainty.rationale: No explicit flags on where things can go wrong — the strict:true/additionalProperties:false merge at function_tool_adapter.rb:21, the empty-required deletion at :63 that could silently affect required params, and the filtering error surface (UndeclaredToolError) are mentioned but never called out as verify-before-you-act risks.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three required adapters appear with file:line (message_adapters/base.rb:17, transcript_adapter.rb:14/26/82, multimodal_content_adapter.rb:20/28/51) and the flow is traceable: normalization at chat_completion.rb:133→139, transcript bridge at :270, multimodal replay at :349–372. An agent could enter at any of these points with only small follow-up reads.

specificity.rationale: Every adapter is named by file and line, with exact method names (transform, add_message_from_hash, flatten, message_to_raix_format, MultimodalContentAdapter.translate) and specific line ranges for each logic branch (cache-control at :42–:45, base64 decode at :51–:61).

justification.rationale: The answer shows WHAT each adapter does in detail but never states WHY the pipeline exists (raw Raix transcript shape doesn't match what RubyLLM/the provider expects, so transforms normalize it); the Anthropic cache-control mention hints at provider-shape normalization but the design rationale is implicit, not stated.

uncertainty.rationale: Conditional paths are described (cache_at branch, image_url arrays, base64 URIs) but none are flagged as verification checkpoints; the answer doesn't warn the agent that provider differences or multimodal vs. text branching are places to confirm before acting.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers every piece the rubric asks for — Raix::MCP::Tool at mcp/tool.rb:11, both transport clients with key methods and line numbers, and the full normalization path through mcp.rb:70–157 that lands MCP tools in self.class.functions and routes them through FunctionToolAdapter and dispatch_tool_function exactly like locals; an agent could open the PR from this map alone.

specificity.rationale: Every component is tied to a concrete file and line: mcp/tool.rb:11/:29, mcp.rb:41/:54/:70/:76/:85–93/:96/:108/:110–117/:120–157, mcp/sse_client.rb:32/:47/:80/:86, mcp/stdio_client.rb:16/:26/:59, and chat_completion.rb:184 — no vague references to 'the MCP code'.

justification.rationale: The answer explains WHY the design works — MCP tools are normalized through the same function DSL (:108) so they land in self.class.functions and are schema-converted and dispatched identically to locals — though it states this conclusion more than it explains the design motivation (one calling path so the model sees a single unified tool list).

uncertainty.rationale: The answer describes where the external path differs (coerce_arguments, remote input_schema overwrite, transport clients) but never explicitly flags these as things the agent should verify before acting; a 'confirm the coercion contract and schema-overwrite behavior' callout is absent.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers every area the rubric names — chat_completion.rb with granular line ranges, function_dispatch.rb + function_tool_adapter.rb, all three adapters, the full MCP stack (mcp.rb, tool.rb, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb), and a wide spec matrix — and adds prompt_declarations.rb as a non-obvious higher-level caller; an agent could open the PR from this list with only small targeted reads.

specificity.rationale: Every site is pinned to concrete file:line ranges (e.g., 'chat_completion.rb:160–219', 'function_dispatch.rb:46–107', 'mcp.rb:70–162') with parenthetical labels on what each range does; nothing is vague like 'the tool code'.

justification.rationale: The section headings ('Chat loop / dispatch', 'Declaration/schema', 'External tools') imply WHY each group is affected, but the answer never states explicitly why a site is load-bearing (e.g., that function_tool_adapter.rb is the only place declarations become the schema the model sees, or that adapters must mirror the contract shape); the parentheticals ('register/dispatch/coerce') hint at it without committing.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents the list as exhaustive with no caveats: it does not flag that mixins are only reached when included in a host class (static reading can miss absent includes), that VCR cassettes require regeneration if the contract wire format changes, or that MCP transport paths differ from local dispatch and need manual verification.

map_quality.rationale: All four areas are mapped with file:line throughout: function_dispatch.rb:46-65/67-106/126-130, function_tool_adapter.rb:6-48, chat_completion.rb:160-219 and sub-lines, mcp.rb:99-158 plus all three MCP sub-files, full adapter set, and a comprehensive spec list including VCR cassettes. The JSON edit_map at the end is immediately actionable; an agent could open every file from it.

specificity.rationale: Every change point is named by exact file and line range (e.g., 'chat_completion.rb:297-299 dispatch_tool_function', 'function_tool_adapter.rb:21 params forwarding, :29 execute') rather than vague area descriptions; the spec section names individual cassette globs and support files like spec/support/mcp_server.rb.

justification.rationale: Ownership roles are explained ('contract source of truth', 'schema-to-RubyLLM::Tool conversion', 'keep MCP in lockstep with A and B'), and the round_trip_order JSON makes the pipeline sequence clear. However, WHY certain adapter files are only conditionally in scope ('only if message/content shape changes') is asserted rather than explained from first principles.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer notes two conditional scopes ('only if message/content shape changes', 'only if call_tool/tools signatures change') but does not explicitly flag where the agent should verify before committing — no callouts around schema format ambiguity, loop-reentry edge cases, or VCR cassette regeneration caveats beyond a passing mention to 'regenerate/extend'.
